articles.title: Over 8,000 Died in Uttar Pradesh's Five-Day Heatwave, More Than Double Bihar and MP's Toll - Dailyhunt
articles.full_text:
New Delhi: At least 8,056 people died during a five-day intense heatwave in Uttar Pradesh, making it India’s deadliest hotspot for heat-related fatalities, according to a study published on May 26. The northern state frequently faces the highest risk of excess deaths during extreme heat events in the country.
The figures in the research are based on estimated fatalities during the 2024 heatwave that broke all heat records, and emerged as the hottest year on the planet since 1901.
The research suggested that even a single day of extreme heat yields thousands of excess deaths, but a multi-day heatwave was deadlier. Uttar Pradesh's toll was more than double that of Bihar, which takes the second place on the list with approximately 3,615 deaths. This is followed by Madhya Pradesh with about 2,964 deaths, Rajasthan (approximately 2,664 deaths) and Gujarat (approximately 2,354 deaths).
Together, these five states alone comprise 43% of India's population.
According to the study, the concentration of heat-related deaths reflects the combined effects of large population size, higher baseline mortality, and exposure to extreme heat conditions.
"The results suggest that heatwave-related mortality is driven not only by climatic intensity but also by underlying demographic vulnerability," it states.
In an earlier report by The Wire, researchers studying heat and impacts noted that while the numbers in the study are much higher than government data, the estimates could still be conservative.
Across India, the study found, a single-day heatwave can cause around 3,400 excess deaths every year, and that number can climb to around 30,000 during a prolonged heatwave, say for five days.
The study used publicly-available district-level population and mortality data (data provided by the Civil Registration System of India for the year 2020), and combined these with a climate classification system to ascertain how heat led to excess deaths in specific climatic zones.
Most vulnerable heat zones
The study also showed that the heatwave impact varied across the state, which shows that it is driven not only by climatic intensity but also by underlying demographic vulnerability.
Uttar Pradesh had the highest number of cities/districts in the heat-vulnerable zones. These included Prayagraj, Patna, Lucknow, Kanpur Nagar, Azamgarh, Agra, and Bareilly, each with excess deaths exceeding 180 during the five-day heatwave.
Across the other states, the death-burden zones were fewer - though with a higher concentration - and included Ahmedabad (Gujarat) with approximately 307 deaths, Jaipur (Rajasthan) approximately 265 deaths, and Surat (Gujarat) approximately 261 deaths.
Where things stand in 2026
In May this year, Banda in Bundelkhand district of Uttar Pradesh, which frequently claims the spot for hottest place in India (and sometimes globally), spent more than a week at the top of the national ranking with temperatures soaring at 47-48°C.
This was extraordinary even by local standards.
Moreover, on May 22, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) had issued a red alert for 10 districts in the state, warning of “severe” to “very severe” heatwave conditions, and orange alert for 34 more districts.
Even for the rest of India, the IMD warned that the intense ongoing heatwave was to continue, issuing alerts on May 25 and 26, with multiple red, orange and yellow heatwave warnings for most parts of the country.
